title: Config file path accepted from CLI and read without any path restriction, enabling read of arbitrary files
description: The --config flag accepts any string and passes it directly to readFile without validating that the path is within an expected directory. An attacker who can control CLI arguments (e.g., via a wrapper script, CI parameter injection, or shell-script composition) can read arbitrary files on the filesystem. The parsed config is then merged into the CLI options object with no schema enforcement before cliArgsSchema.parse, so a crafted config file could inject unexpected option values before Zod sees them.

title: --rm accepts arbitrary imageId string and joins it directly into filesystem paths without sanitization
description: The rm subcommand accepts a target from CLI positionals or --rm flag and performs a path-include check (contains '/', '\', or '.') to decide whether to treat it as a path or an imageId. If the target looks like an imageId (no separators/dots), it is joined directly: join(outDir, 'images', imageId, ...). A value like '../../etc' passes the separator check and is used as an imageId segment without validation. However, the imageId pattern check only applies to the explain subcommand; rm's imageId is not regex-validated against /^[0-9a-f]{16}$/ before use in path joins, so crafted values can escape outDir.

title: Level contenthash from manifest used in filesystem path without validation against expected hex format
description: In the validate function, each lv.contenthash is joined directly into a filesystem path as join(levelsDir, lv.contenthash + '.jxl'). The schema enforces z.string().length(16) but does not enforce that only hex characters are present (the regex /^[0-9a-f]{16}$/ is only used for imageId). A manifest with a contenthash like '../../../etc/shadow' padded or truncated to 16 characters could traverse out of the levels directory. The same pattern exists in ingest.ts writeLevelFiles.

title: contenthash used to construct level file paths without hex-character validation
description: writeLevelFiles joins entry.contenthash directly into the levelsDir path without checking that the hash contains only hex characters. The Zod schema for contenthash enforces length(16) but not character set. A crafted manifest (introduced via corrupt/adversarial manifest file) containing a contenthash with path-separator characters could cause level files to be written outside the intended levels directory. The removeOrphans and gc code paths also build paths from manifest contenthashes in the same unvalidated way.

title: imageId used as path component in acquireImageWriteLock without hex validation
description: acquireImageWriteLock constructs the lock path as join(outDir, 'images', imageId, '.lock'). The imageId is not validated against /^[0-9a-f]{16}$/ at the call site in lock.ts; it is the caller's responsibility. If an adversarially crafted imageId (e.g., supplied via --rm or from a manipulated idMap in worker messages) contains path separators, the lock file could be placed outside the gallery directory. This is a defense-in-depth gap; the imageId regex is only enforced by the manifest Zod schema, not at lock acquisition.

title: 64-bit truncated SHA-256 used as content-addressed storage key risks birthday collision at ~2B objects
description: contentHash and imageIdForPath both use only the first 16 hex characters (64 bits) of SHA-256. The birthday bound for a 50% collision probability is ~2^32 objects (~4 billion). For a biodiversity platform ingesting millions of specimen images over years this is a real operational concern: a collision would cause one image's level files to silently serve another image. The code comment acknowledges this and suggests 96-bit (24 hex), but the default remains 64-bit with no migration path enforced. This is not an immediate exploitable vulnerability but a structural integrity risk as the corpus grows.

title: Full CLI argv (including sensitive flags and paths) written to world-readable runlog file
description: The runlog record at .pyramid-ingest.runlog.json includes args: process.argv.slice(2), which captures the complete command line including --out path, --config path, and any other flags that may encode environment-specific or sensitive information. The file is written without any permissions restriction beyond the OS default umask. On a shared system or in a container where the output directory is mounted or readable by other users, the runlog exposes operational metadata. GPS coordinates are also present in EXIF metadata extracted from images, which could be written into the manifest metadata field.

title: GPS coordinates extracted and stored in manifest metadata by default; opt-out is not the default
description: extractBasicMetadata is called with gps = !opts.stripGps, meaning GPS data is included by default unless the caller passes stripGps: true. For a biodiversity platform documenting sensitive species locations, the default should be GPS-off with explicit opt-in. The IngestOptions interface documents stripGps as a privacy option but it is never set to true in the CLI parsing code—there is no --strip-gps flag exposed to users. GPS coordinates stored in manifests and the gallery index are served to all clients.

title: 512 MB master file size limit is checked only after reading the file into memory
description: MAX_MASTER_BYTES = 512 * 1024 * 1024 is checked against info.size (from stat) before the readFile call, so a file that grows between stat and read can exceed the limit in memory. More importantly, the stat-then-read pattern is a TOCTOU: a symlink or special file could return a small stat size but deliver arbitrary data on read. However, on typical deployments this is a local file system operation and the risk is low. A separate concern is that the 512 MB limit is high enough that a single malicious master file can consume significant RAM during the decode + RGBA buffer allocation phase.

title: contenthash field in levelEntrySchema should enforce hex-only character set, not just length
description: The levelEntrySchema uses z.string().length(16) for contenthash but does not add a regex constraint for [0-9a-f]{16}. Since contenthash values are used to construct filesystem paths (join(levelsDir, contenthash + '.jxl')), enforcing the character set at the Zod layer would prevent any crafted manifest from introducing path-traversal characters into storage operations. Adding .regex(/^[0-9a-f]{16}$/) mirrors the existing imageId validation and closes the gap for any manifest that enters the system from an untrusted source (e.g., network share, migrated store).
code_excerpt:
export const levelEntrySchema = z.object({
  size: levelSizeSchema,
  w: z.number().int().positive(),
  h: z.number().int().positive(),
  bytes: z.number().int().nonnegative(),
  bitsPerSample: z.union([z.literal(8), z.literal(16)]),
  contenthash: z.string().length(16),
  tiled: z.boolean(),

title: master.name field has no length or character-set constraint, allowing oversized or control-character filenames in manifests
description: masterInfoSchema validates format and mtimeMs but places no constraint on name beyond being a non-empty string. A crafted manifest could embed a name with null bytes, path separators, or an arbitrarily long string. While name is not currently joined into filesystem paths (imageId is used for directory naming), it is written verbatim into manifests and could cause issues if future code uses name for filesystem operations, or if name is rendered into user-facing output without sanitization (the explain subcommand prints it directly to stdout).

title: exifr is dynamically imported with no version pinning enforcement or integrity check
description: exifr is loaded via dynamic import with a catch-null fallback, so a missing or compromised exifr package silently degrades to Tier 5 rather than failing loudly. The package processes attacker-controlled binary files (RAW/JPEG) and extracts EXIF data including GPS coordinates. A supply-chain compromise of exifr would allow arbitrary code execution during the metadata extraction phase for every ingested image. There is no indication of a dependency audit step in CI (no audit script visible in package.json). Pinning exifr to an exact version with integrity checking in bun.lock and running bun audit in CI would reduce this risk.
